title: Cutting and baling hay: a small-scale guide
subtitle: What a smallholder needs to understand about making hay from a small field.
excerpt: Making hay on a small holding requires a contractor with equipment, a three to four day weather window, and knowing when to cut. This is a guide to the decisions involved: when to cut, how to judge dryness before baling, what to ask from the contractor, and how to store small bales.
sourceNotes: AHDB Grass and Forage, cutting date guidance. NIAB, grass varieties and cutting guide. Farmers Weekly, small-scale hay making. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (10th edition), hay-making chapter.

content.text: Most smallholders with less than 2 hectares of grass manage hay by hiring a contractor with a mower,

, and baler. The smallholder's role is to identify the cutting window, communicate with the contractor, and have the storage ready for the bales. The decisions and timing are where the smallholder adds value.

In the UK, the standard first-cut window for hay is late May to late June, when grasses have reached their highest dry matter yield before seed heads have fully passed. Cutting earlier gives softer, more palatable hay; cutting later gives higher yield but lower digestibility. The practical constraint is weather: you need a settled forecast of three to four dry days with reasonable warmth after cutting. Book the contractor in advance with a target week in mind, understanding that you will proceed on the best weather window in that period.

Baling before the grass is fully dry results in mouldy, hot bales that can cause respiratory problems in stock. Test dryness by twisting a handful of hay: if moisture is visible in the twist, it is not ready. The stems, not just the leaves, must be dry; grass stems dry more slowly than the surface of the windrow appears to indicate. A moisture content below 20% is the target for safe storage; a simple hay moisture probe confirms this.
A field cut in early June can produce a

in late August or September, given sufficient recovery growth. Second cut is not always worth the contractor cost on very small areas; weigh the bale yield against the fixed contractor cost before committing.

Small square bales need storage off the ground on pallets or a concrete floor with air circulation. Stack with the cut ends outward to allow remaining moisture to continue escaping. A dry barn is ideal; an outdoor stack must be covered with a waterproof sheet tied down securely. Bales stored correctly at the right moisture content keep for 18 months to two years.
